title: Painting

infobox.value: is a [visual art](/wiki/Visual_arts "Visual arts"), characterized by the practice of applying [paint](/wiki/Paint "Paint"), [pigment](/wiki/Pigment "Pigment"), [color](/wiki/Color "Color") or other medium to a solid surface.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The medium is commonly applied to the base with a [brush](/wiki/Brush "Brush"). Other implements, such as palette knives, sponges, [airbrushes](/wiki/Airbrush "Airbrush"), the artist's fingers, or even a dripping technique that uses gravity may be used. One who produces paintings is called a **painter**.

Practice of applying paint, pigment, color or other medium to a surface

This article is about artistic painting. For other uses, see [Painting (disambiguation)](/wiki/Painting_(disambiguation) "Painting (disambiguation)"). "Painter" redirects here. For other uses, see [Painter (disambiguation)](/wiki/Painter_(disambiguation) "Painter (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Mona_Lisa%2C_by_Leonardo_da_Vinci%2C_from_C2RMF_retouched.jpg/250px-Mona_Lisa%2C_by_Leonardo_da_Vinci%2C_from_C2RMF_retouched.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mona_Lisa,_by_Leonardo_da_Vinci,_from_C2RMF_retouched.jpg)

*[Mona Lisa](/wiki/Mona_Lisa "Mona Lisa")* (1503–1517) by [Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci") is one of the world's most recognizable paintings.

**Painting** is a [visual art](/wiki/Visual_arts "Visual arts"), characterized by the practice of applying [paint](/wiki/Paint "Paint"), [pigment](/wiki/Pigment "Pigment"), [color](/wiki/Color "Color") or other medium to a solid surface.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The medium is commonly applied to the base with a [brush](/wiki/Brush "Brush"). Other implements, such as palette knives, sponges, [airbrushes](/wiki/Airbrush "Airbrush"), the artist's fingers, or even a dripping technique that uses gravity may be used. One who produces paintings is called a **painter**.

In [art](/wiki/Art "Art"), the term "painting" describes both the act and the result of the action (the final work is called "a painting"). The support for paintings includes such surfaces as walls, paper, canvas, wood, glass, [lacquer](/wiki/Lacquer "Lacquer"), pottery, [leaf](/wiki/Leaf "Leaf"), copper and [concrete](/wiki/Concrete "Concrete"); the painting may incorporate other materials, in single or multiple form, including sand, [clay](/wiki/Clay "Clay"), paper, cardboard, newspaper, [plaster](/wiki/Plaster "Plaster"), [gold leaf](/wiki/Gold_leaf "Gold leaf"), and even entire objects.

Painting is an important form of [visual art](/wiki/Visual_arts "Visual arts"), bringing in elements such as [drawing](/wiki/Drawing "Drawing"), [composition](/wiki/Composition_(visual_arts) "Composition (visual arts)"), [gesture](/wiki/Gestural_painting "Gestural painting"), [narration](/wiki/Narrative_art "Narrative art"), and [abstraction](/wiki/Abstract_art "Abstract art").[[2]](#cite_note-2) Paintings can be naturalistic and representational (as in [portraits](/wiki/Portrait "Portrait"), [still life](/wiki/Still_life "Still life") and [landscape painting](/wiki/Landscape_art "Landscape art")--though these genres can also be abstract), [photographic](/wiki/Photorealism "Photorealism"), abstract, narrative, [symbolist](/wiki/Symbol "Symbol") (as in [Symbolist art](/wiki/Symbolism_(arts) "Symbolism (arts)")), [emotive](/wiki/Emotion "Emotion") (as in [Expressionism](/wiki/Expressionism "Expressionism")) or [political](/wiki/Politics "Politics") in nature (as in [Artivism](/wiki/Artivism "Artivism")).

A significant share of the [history of painting](/wiki/History_of_painting "History of painting") in both Eastern and Western art is dominated by [religious art](/wiki/Religious_art "Religious art"). Examples of this kind of painting range from artwork depicting [mythological](/wiki/Mythology "Mythology") figures on [pottery](/wiki/Pottery "Pottery"), to [Biblical](/wiki/Bible "Bible") scenes on the [Sistine Chapel ceiling](/wiki/Sistine_Chapel_ceiling "Sistine Chapel ceiling"), to scenes from the life of [Buddha](/wiki/Chinese_painting "Chinese painting") (or other images of [Eastern religious origin](/wiki/Eastern_art_history "Eastern art history")).

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of painting](/wiki/History_of_painting "History of painting")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Pig-deer_print_paintings_in_Pettakere_Cave%2C_Maros.jpg/250px-Pig-deer_print_paintings_in_Pettakere_Cave%2C_Maros.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pig-deer_print_paintings_in_Pettakere_Cave,_Maros.jpg)

Cave paintings depicting a wild boar hunt in the [Maros-Pangkep karst](/wiki/Caves_in_the_Maros-Pangkep_karst "Caves in the Maros-Pangkep karst") of Sulawesi are estimated to be at least 43,900 years old (2014). This finding was recognized as "the oldest known depiction of [storytelling](/wiki/Storytelling "Storytelling") and the earliest instance of figurative art in human history."

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Maros_hunting_scene.png/250px-Maros_hunting_scene.png)](/wiki/File:Maros_hunting_scene.png)

Redrawing of hunting scene from the Caves in the Maros-Pangkep karst

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Lubang_Jeriji_Sal%C3%A9h_cave_painting_of_Bull.jpg/250px-Lubang_Jeriji_Sal%C3%A9h_cave_painting_of_Bull.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lubang_Jeriji_Sal%C3%A9h_cave_painting_of_Bull.jpg)

The depiction of a bull found in the [Lubang Jeriji Saleh](/wiki/Lubang_Jeriji_Saleh "Lubang Jeriji Saleh"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"), in 2018, is the world's oldest known figurative painting. The painting is estimated to have been created around 40,000 to 52,000 years ago, or even earlier.

The oldest known paintings are more than 40,000-60,000 years old ([art of the Upper Paleolithic](/wiki/Art_of_the_Upper_Paleolithic "Art of the Upper Paleolithic")) and found in the [caves in the district of Maros](/wiki/Caves_in_the_district_of_Maros "Caves in the district of Maros") ([Sulawesi](/wiki/Sulawesi "Sulawesi"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia")). The oldest are often constructed from hand stencils and simple geometric shapes.[[3]](#cite_note-Aubert2014-3)[[a]](#cite_note-5)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Leang_Tedongnge_rock_art_panel_credit_AA_Oktaviana-01.jpg/250px-Leang_Tedongnge_rock_art_panel_credit_AA_Oktaviana-01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leang_Tedongnge_rock_art_panel_credit_AA_Oktaviana-01.jpg)

In 2021, researchers discovered ancient cave art in Leang Tedongnge, Sulawesi, Indonesia, estimated to be at least 45,500 years old. Depicting a warty pig, this artwork is recognized as the world's oldest known example of figurative or representational art.

In November 2018, scientists reported the discovery of the then-oldest known figurative art painting, over 40,000 (perhaps as old as 52,000) years old, of an unknown animal, in the cave of [Lubang Jeriji Saléh](/wiki/Lubang_Jeriji_Sal%C3%A9h "Lubang Jeriji Saléh") on the [Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") island of [Borneo](/wiki/Borneo "Borneo").[[5]](#cite_note-NYT-20181107-cz-6)[[6]](#cite_note-NAT-20181107-7) In December 2019, cave paintings portraying pig hunting within the [Maros-Pangkep karst](/wiki/Caves_in_the_Maros-Pangkep_karst "Caves in the Maros-Pangkep karst") region in [Sulawesi](/wiki/Sulawesi "Sulawesi") were discovered to be even older, with an estimated age of at least 43,900 years. This finding was recognized as "the oldest known depiction of [storytelling](/wiki/Storytelling "Storytelling") and the earliest instance of figurative art in human history."[[7]](#cite_note-8)[[8]](#cite_note-NYT-20191211-9) In 2021, cave art of a pig found in [Sulawesi](/wiki/Sulawesi "Sulawesi"), Indonesia, and dated to over 45,500 years ago, has been reported.[[9]](#cite_note-10)[[10]](#cite_note-NYT-20210113-11) On July 3, 2024, the journal *[Nature](/wiki/Nature_(journal) "Nature (journal)")* published research findings indicating that the cave paintings which depict [anthropomorphic](/wiki/Anthropomorphism "Anthropomorphism") figures interacting with a pig and measure 36 by 15 inches (91 by 38 cm) in [Leang Karampuang](/wiki/Karampuang_cave "Karampuang cave") are approximately 51,200 years old, establishing them as the oldest known paintings in the world.[[11]](#cite_note-:0-12)[[12]](#cite_note-:1-13)

There are examples of [cave paintings](/wiki/Cave_painting "Cave painting") all over the world—in [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"), [France](/wiki/France "France"), [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"), [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal"), [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), [China](/wiki/China "China"), [India](/wiki/Bhimbetka_rock_shelters "Bhimbetka rock shelters"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"),[[13]](#cite_note-14) etc. In Western cultures, [oil painting](/wiki/Oil_painting "Oil painting") and [watercolor](/wiki/Watercolor "Watercolor") painting have rich and complex traditions in style and subject matter. In the East, [ink](/wiki/Ink "Ink") and color ink historically predominated the choice of media, with equally rich and complex traditions.

The invention of photography had a major impact on painting. In the decades after the first [photograph](/wiki/Photograph "Photograph") was produced in 1829, [photographic](/wiki/Photography "Photography") processes improved and became more widely practiced, depriving painting of much of its historic purpose to provide an accurate record of the observable world. A series of art movements in the late 19th and early 20th centuries—notably [Impressionism](/wiki/Impressionism "Impressionism"), [Post-Impressionism](/wiki/Post-Impressionism "Post-Impressionism"), [Fauvism](/wiki/Fauvism "Fauvism"), [Expressionism](/wiki/Expressionism "Expressionism"), [Cubism](/wiki/Cubism "Cubism"), and [Dadaism](/wiki/Dada "Dada")—challenged the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance_art "Renaissance art") view of the world. Eastern and African painting, however, continued a long history of [stylization](/wiki/Stylization "Stylization") and did not undergo an equivalent transformation at the same time.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Modern](/wiki/Modern_art "Modern art") and [Contemporary art](/wiki/Contemporary_art "Contemporary art") has moved away from the historic value of craft and documentation in favour of [concept](/wiki/Concept "Concept"). This has not deterred the majority of living painters from continuing to practice painting either as a whole or part of their work. The vitality and versatility of painting in the 21st century defy the previous "declarations" of its demise. In an epoch characterized by the idea of [pluralism](/wiki/Cultural_pluralism "Cultural pluralism"), there is no consensus as to a representative style of the age. Artists continue to make important works of art in a wide variety of styles and aesthetic [temperaments](/wiki/Temperament "Temperament")—their merits are left to the public and the marketplace to judge.

[The Feminist art movement](/wiki/Feminist_art_movement "Feminist art movement")[[14]](#cite_note-15) began in the 1960s during the second wave of [feminism](/wiki/Feminism "Feminism"). The movement sought to gain equal rights and equal opportunities for female artists internationally.

### Color and tone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Color and tone")]

[Color](/wiki/Color "Color"), made up of [hue](/wiki/Hue "Hue"), [saturation](/wiki/Saturation_color "Saturation color"), and [value](/wiki/Value_(color) "Value (color)"), dispersed over a surface is the essence of painting, just as [pitch](/wiki/Pitch_(music) "Pitch (music)") and [rhythm](/wiki/Rhythm "Rhythm") are the essence of [music](/wiki/Music "Music"). Color is highly subjective, but has observable psychological effects, although these can differ from one culture to the next. Black is associated with mourning in the West, but in the East, white is. Some painters, theoreticians, writers, and scientists, including [Goethe](/wiki/Johann_Wolfgang_Goethe "Johann Wolfgang Goethe"),[[15]](#cite_note-16) [Kandinsky](/wiki/Wassily_Kandinsky "Wassily Kandinsky"),[[16]](#cite_note-17) and [Newton](/wiki/Isaac_Newton "Isaac Newton"),[[17]](#cite_note-18) have written their own [color theory](/wiki/Color_theory "Color theory").

Moreover, the use of language is only an abstraction of color equivalent. The word "[red](/wiki/Red "Red")", for example, can cover a wide range of variations from the pure red of the [visible spectrum](/wiki/Visible_spectrum "Visible spectrum") of light. There is not a formalized register of different colors in the way that there is agreement on different notes in music, such as [F](/wiki/F_(musical_note) "F (musical note)") or [C♯](/wiki/C%E2%99%AF_(musical_note) "C♯ (musical note)"). For a painter, color is not simply divided into basic (primary) and derived (complementary or mixed) colors (like red, blue, green, brown, etc.).

Painters deal practically with [pigments](/wiki/Pigments "Pigments"),[[18]](#cite_note-19) so "[blue](/wiki/Blue "Blue")" for a painter can be any of the blues: [phthalocyanine blue](/wiki/Phthalocyanine_blue "Phthalocyanine blue"), [Prussian blue](/wiki/Prussian_blue "Prussian blue"), [indigo](/wiki/Indigo "Indigo"), [Cobalt blue](/wiki/Cobalt_blue "Cobalt blue"), [ultramarine](/wiki/Ultramarine "Ultramarine"), and so on. Psychological and symbolical meanings of color are not, strictly speaking, means of painting. Colors only add to the potential, derived context of meanings, and because of this, the perception of a painting is highly subjective. The analogy with music is quite clear—sound in music (like a C note) is analogous to "light" in painting, "shades" to [dynamics](/wiki/Dynamics_(music) "Dynamics (music)"), and "coloration" is to painting as the specific [timbre](/wiki/Tone_color "Tone color") of musical instruments is to music. These elements do not necessarily form a melody (in music) of themselves; rather, they can add different contexts to it.

### Non-traditional elements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Non-traditional elements")]

Modern artists have extended the practice of painting considerably to include, as one example, [collage](/wiki/Collage "Collage"), which began with [Cubism](/wiki/Cubism "Cubism") and is not painting in the strict sense. Some modern painters incorporate different materials such as metal, plastic, [sand](/wiki/Sand "Sand"), [cement](/wiki/Cement "Cement"), [straw](/wiki/Straw "Straw"), [leaves](/wiki/Leaf_painting "Leaf painting") or [wood](/wiki/Wood "Wood") for the texture. Examples of this are the works of [Jean Dubuffet](/wiki/Jean_Dubuffet "Jean Dubuffet") and [Anselm Kiefer](/wiki/Anselm_Kiefer "Anselm Kiefer"). There is a growing community of artists who use computers to "paint" color onto a digital "canvas" using programs such as [Adobe Photoshop](/wiki/Adobe_Photoshop "Adobe Photoshop"), [Corel Painter](/wiki/Corel_Painter "Corel Painter"), and many others. These images can be printed onto traditional canvas if required.

### Rhythm

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Rhythm")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/d/d6/Jean_Metzinger%2C_1906%2C_La_dance_%28Bacchante%29%2C_oil_on_canvas%2C_73_x_54_cm_DSC05359...jpg/250px-Jean_Metzinger%2C_1906%2C_La_dance_%28Bacchante%29%2C_oil_on_canvas%2C_73_x_54_cm_DSC05359...jpg)](/wiki/File:Jean_Metzinger,_1906,_La_dance_(Bacchante),_oil_on_canvas,_73_x_54_cm_DSC05359...jpg)

[Jean Metzinger](/wiki/Jean_Metzinger "Jean Metzinger"), *[La danse (Bacchante)](/wiki/La_danse,_Bacchante "La danse, Bacchante")* (c. 1906), oil on canvas, 73 x 54 cm, [Kröller-Müller Museum](/wiki/Kr%C3%B6ller-M%C3%BCller_Museum "Kröller-Müller Museum")

[Jean Metzinger](/wiki/Jean_Metzinger "Jean Metzinger")'s mosaic-like [Divisionist](/wiki/Divisionist "Divisionist") technique had its parallel in literature; a characteristic of the alliance between [Symbolist](/wiki/Symbolism_(movement) "Symbolism (movement)") writers and Neo-Impressionist artists:

> I ask of divided brushwork not the objective rendering of light, but iridescences and certain aspects of color still foreign to painting. I make a kind of chromatic versification and for syllables, I use strokes which, variable in quantity, cannot differ in dimension without modifying the rhythm of a pictorial phraseology destined to translate the diverse emotions aroused by nature. (Jean Metzinger, c. 1907)[[19]](#cite_note-20)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Piet_Mondriaan%2C_1921_-_Composition_en_rouge%2C_jaune%2C_bleu_et_noir.jpg/250px-Piet_Mondriaan%2C_1921_-_Composition_en_rouge%2C_jaune%2C_bleu_et_noir.jpg)](/wiki/File:Piet_Mondriaan,_1921_-_Composition_en_rouge,_jaune,_bleu_et_noir.jpg)

[Piet Mondrian](/wiki/Piet_Mondrian "Piet Mondrian"), *Composition en rouge, jaune, bleu et noir* (1921), [Gemeentemuseum Den Haag](/wiki/Gemeentemuseum_Den_Haag "Gemeentemuseum Den Haag")

[Rhythm](/wiki/Rhythm "Rhythm"), for artists such as [Piet Mondrian](/wiki/Piet_Mondrian "Piet Mondrian"),[[20]](#cite_note-Tosaki-21)[[21]](#cite_note-22) is important in painting as it is in music. If one defines rhythm as "a pause incorporated into a sequence", then there can be rhythm in paintings. These pauses allow creative force to intervene and add new creations—form, melody, coloration. The distribution of form or any kind of information is of crucial importance in the given work of art, and it directly affects the aesthetic value of that work. This is because the aesthetic value is functionality dependent, i.e. the freedom (of movement) of perception is perceived as beauty. Free flow of energy, in art as well as in other forms of "[techne](/wiki/Techne "Techne")", directly contributes to the aesthetic value.[[20]](#cite_note-Tosaki-21)

Music was important to the birth of [abstract art](/wiki/Abstract_art "Abstract art") since music is abstract by nature—it does not try to represent the exterior world, but expresses in an immediate way the inner feelings of the soul. [Wassily Kandinsky](/wiki/Wassily_Kandinsky "Wassily Kandinsky") often used musical terms to identify his works; he called his most spontaneous paintings "improvisations" and described more elaborate works as "compositions". Kandinsky theorized that "music is the ultimate teacher",[[22]](#cite_note-23) and subsequently embarked upon the first seven of his ten *Compositions*. Hearing tones and chords as he painted, Kandinsky theorized that (for example), yellow is the color of middle [C](/wiki/C_(musical_note) "C (musical note)") on a brassy trumpet; black is the color of closure, and the end of things; and that combinations of colors produce vibrational frequencies, akin to chords played on a piano. In 1871 the young Kandinsky learned to play the piano and cello.[[23]](#cite_note-24)[[24]](#cite_note-25) Kandinsky's stage design for a performance of [Mussorgsky](/wiki/Modest_Mussorgsky "Modest Mussorgsky")'s *[Pictures at an Exhibition](/wiki/Pictures_at_an_Exhibition "Pictures at an Exhibition")* illustrates his "synaesthetic" concept of a universal correspondence of forms, colors and musical sounds.[[25]](#cite_note-26)

Music defines much of modernist abstract painting. [Jackson Pollock](/wiki/Jackson_Pollock "Jackson Pollock") underscores that interest with his 1950 painting *[Autumn Rhythm (Number 30)](/wiki/Autumn_Rhythm_(Number_30) "Autumn Rhythm (Number 30)")*.[[26]](#cite_note-27)

## Aesthetics and theory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Aesthetics and theory")]

Main article: [Theory of painting](/wiki/Theory_of_painting "Theory of painting")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Pompeii_Painter.jpg/250px-Pompeii_Painter.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pompeii_Painter.jpg)

Female painter sitting on a campstool and painting a statue of [Dionysus](/wiki/Dionysus "Dionysus") or [Priapus](/wiki/Priapus "Priapus") onto a panel which is held by a boy. Fresco from [Pompeii](/wiki/Pompeii "Pompeii"), 1st century

[Aesthetics](/wiki/Aesthetics "Aesthetics") is the study of [art](/wiki/Art "Art") and [beauty](/wiki/Beauty "Beauty"); it was an important issue for 18th- and 19th-century [philosophers](/wiki/Philosophy "Philosophy") such as [Kant](/wiki/Immanuel_Kant "Immanuel Kant") and [Hegel](/wiki/Hegel "Hegel"). Classical philosophers like [Plato](/wiki/Plato "Plato") and [Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle") also theorized about art and painting in particular. Plato disregarded painters (as well as sculptors) in his philosophical system; he maintained that painting cannot depict the [truth](/wiki/Truth "Truth")—it is a copy of reality (a shadow of the world of ideas) and is nothing but a [craft](/wiki/Craft "Craft"), similar to shoemaking or iron casting.[[27]](#cite_note-28) By the time of Leonardo, painting had become a closer representation of the truth than painting was in [Ancient Greece](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece"). [Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci"), on the contrary, said that "[Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *La Pittura è cosa mentale*" ("English: painting is a thing of the mind").[[28]](#cite_note-29) Kant distinguished between [Beauty](/wiki/Beauty "Beauty") and the [Sublime](/wiki/Sublime_(philosophy) "Sublime (philosophy)"), in terms that clearly gave priority to the former.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Although he did not refer to painting in particular, this concept was taken up by painters such as [J.M.W. Turner](/wiki/Joseph_Mallord_William_Turner "Joseph Mallord William Turner") and [Caspar David Friedrich](/wiki/Caspar_David_Friedrich "Caspar David Friedrich").

[![A relief against a wall shows a bearded man reaching up with his hands as his clothes are draped over his body.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Formella_18%2C_apelle_o_la_pittura%2C_nino_pisano%2C_1334-1336_dettaglio_01.JPG/250px-Formella_18%2C_apelle_o_la_pittura%2C_nino_pisano%2C_1334-1336_dettaglio_01.JPG)](/wiki/File:Formella_18,_apelle_o_la_pittura,_nino_pisano,_1334-1336_dettaglio_01.JPG)

[Nino Pisano](/wiki/Nino_Pisano "Nino Pisano"), *Apelles or the Art of painting* in detail (1334–1336); relief of the [Giotto's Bell Tower](/wiki/Giotto%27s_Bell_Tower "Giotto's Bell Tower") in [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence"), Italy

Hegel recognized the failure of attaining a universal concept of beauty and, in his aesthetic essay, wrote that painting is one of the three "romantic" arts, along with [Poetry](/wiki/Poetry "Poetry") and [Music](/wiki/Music "Music"), for its [symbolic](/wiki/Symbol "Symbol"), highly intellectual purpose.[[29]](#cite_note-30)[[30]](#cite_note-31) Painters who have written theoretical works on painting include [Kandinsky](/wiki/Wassily_Kandinsky "Wassily Kandinsky") and [Paul Klee](/wiki/Paul_Klee "Paul Klee").[[31]](#cite_note-Franciscono-32)[[32]](#cite_note-Barasch-33) In his essay, Kandinsky maintains that painting has a spiritual value, and he attaches [primary colors](/wiki/Primary_colors "Primary colors") to essential feelings or concepts, something that [Goethe](/wiki/Goethe "Goethe") and other writers had already tried to do.

[Iconography](/wiki/Iconography "Iconography") is the study of the content of paintings, rather than their style. [Erwin Panofsky](/wiki/Erwin_Panofsky "Erwin Panofsky") and other [art historians](/wiki/Art_historian "Art historian") first seek to understand the things depicted, before looking at their meaning for the viewer at the time, and finally analyzing their wider cultural, religious, and social meaning.[[33]](#cite_note-34)

In 1890, the Parisian painter [Maurice Denis](/wiki/Maurice_Denis "Maurice Denis") famously asserted: "Remember that a painting—before being a warhorse, a naked woman or some story or other—is essentially a flat surface covered with colors assembled in a certain order."[[34]](#cite_note-35) Thus, many 20th-century developments in painting, such as [Cubism](/wiki/Cubism "Cubism"), were reflections on the *means* of painting rather than on the external world—[nature](/wiki/Nature "Nature")—which had previously been its core subject. Recent contributions to thinking about painting have been offered by the painter and writer Julian Bell. In his book *What is Painting?*, Bell discusses the development, through history, of the notion that paintings can express feelings and ideas.[[35]](#cite_note-36) In *Mirror of The World,* Bell writes:

> A *work* of art seeks to hold your attention and keep it fixed: a *history* of art urges it onwards, bulldozing a highway through the homes of the imagination.[[36]](#cite_note-37)

## Painting media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Painting media")]

Different types of paint are usually identified by the medium that the pigment is suspended or embedded in, which determines the general working characteristics of the paint, such as [viscosity](/wiki/Viscosity "Viscosity"), [miscibility](/wiki/Miscibility "Miscibility"), [solubility](/wiki/Solubility "Solubility"), drying time, etc.

### Hot wax or encaustic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Hot wax or encaustic")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Petersinai.jpg/250px-Petersinai.jpg)](/wiki/File:Petersinai.jpg)

Encaustic icon from [Saint Catherine's Monastery](/wiki/Saint_Catherine%27s_Monastery "Saint Catherine's Monastery"), [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") (6th-century)

[Encaustic painting](/wiki/Encaustic_painting "Encaustic painting"), also known as hot wax painting, involves using heated [beeswax](/wiki/Beeswax "Beeswax") to which colored pigments are added. The liquid/paste is then applied to a surface—usually prepared wood, though [canvas](/wiki/Canvas "Canvas") and other materials are often used. The simplest encaustic mixture can be made from adding pigments to beeswax, but there are several other recipes that can be used—some containing other types of [waxes](/wiki/Wax "Wax"), [damar resin](/wiki/Damar_resin "Damar resin"), [linseed oil](/wiki/Linseed_oil "Linseed oil"), or other ingredients. Pure, powdered pigments can be purchased and used, though some mixtures use oil paints or other forms of pigment. Metal tools and special brushes can be used to shape the paint before it cools, or heated metal tools can be used to manipulate the wax once it has cooled onto the surface. Other materials can be encased or [collaged](/wiki/Collage "Collage") into the surface, or layered, using the encaustic medium to adhere it to the surface.

The technique was the normal one for ancient Greek and Roman panel paintings, and remained in use in the Eastern Orthodox [icon](/wiki/Icon "Icon") tradition.

### Watercolor

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Watercolor")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/John_Martin_-_Manfred_on_the_Jungfrau_%281837%29.jpg/250px-John_Martin_-_Manfred_on_the_Jungfrau_%281837%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Martin_-_Manfred_on_the_Jungfrau_(1837).jpg)

[John Martin](/wiki/John_Martin_(painter) "John Martin (painter)"), *[Manfred on the Jungfrau](/wiki/Manfred_on_the_Jungfrau_(Martin) "Manfred on the Jungfrau (Martin)")* (1837), watercolor

[Watercolor](/wiki/Watercolor "Watercolor") is a painting method in which the paints are made of pigments suspended in a water-soluble vehicle. The traditional and most common support for watercolor paintings is paper; other supports include [papyrus](/wiki/Papyrus "Papyrus"), bark papers, plastics, [vellum](/wiki/Vellum "Vellum") or [leather](/wiki/Leather "Leather"), [fabric](/wiki/Textile "Textile"), wood and [canvas](/wiki/Canvas "Canvas"). In East Asia, watercolor painting with inks is referred to as [brush painting](/wiki/Brush_painting "Brush painting") or scroll painting. In [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_painting "Chinese painting"), [Korean](/wiki/Korean_painting "Korean painting"), and [Japanese painting](/wiki/Japanese_painting "Japanese painting") it has been the dominant medium, often in monochrome black or browns. India, [Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopia "Ethiopia") and other countries also have long traditions. [Finger-painting](/wiki/Finger-painting "Finger-painting") with watercolor paints originated in [China](/wiki/China "China"). There are various types of watercolors used by artists. Some examples are pan watercolors, liquid watercolors, watercolor brush pens, and [watercolor pencils](/wiki/Pencil "Pencil"). Watercolor pencils (water-soluble color pencils) may be used either wet or dry.

### Gouache

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Gouache")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Rudolf_Reschreiter_Blick_von_der_H%C3%B6llentalangerh%C3%BCtte_zum_H%C3%B6llentalgletscher_und_den_Riffelwandspitzen_1921.jpg/250px-Rudolf_Reschreiter_Blick_von_der_H%C3%B6llentalangerh%C3%BCtte_zum_H%C3%B6llentalgletscher_und_den_Riffelwandspitzen_1921.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rudolf_Reschreiter_Blick_von_der_H%C3%B6llentalangerh%C3%BCtte_zum_H%C3%B6llentalgletscher_und_den_Riffelwandspitzen_1921.jpg)

[Rudolf Reschreiter](/wiki/Rudolf_Reschreiter "Rudolf Reschreiter"), *Blick von der Höllentalangerhütte zum Höllentalgletscher und den Riffelwandspitzen*, Gouache (1921)

[Gouache](/wiki/Gouache "Gouache") is a water-based paint consisting of pigment and other materials designed to be used in an opaque painting method. Gouache differs from [watercolor](/wiki/Watercolor_painting "Watercolor painting") in that the particles are larger, the ratio of pigment to water is much higher, and an additional, inert, white pigment such as [chalk](/wiki/Chalk "Chalk") is also present. This makes gouache heavier and more opaque, with greater reflective qualities. Like all water media, it is diluted with water.[[37]](#cite_note-wash_gou-38)
Gouache was a popular paint utilized by Egyptians,[[38]](#cite_note-39) Painters such as [Francois Boucher](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Boucher "François Boucher") used this medium. This paint is best applied with sable brushes.

### Ceramic Glaze

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Ceramic Glaze")]

Glazing is commonly known as a premelted liquid glass. This glaze can be dipped or brushed on. This glaze appears chalky and there is a vast difference between the beginning and finished result. To be activated glazed pottery must be placed in a kiln to be fired. This melts the Silica glass in the glaze and transforms it into a vibrant glossy version of itself.[[39]](#cite_note-40)[[40]](#cite_note-41)

### Ink

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Ink")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Landscapes_of_the_Four_Seasons.jpg/250px-Landscapes_of_the_Four_Seasons.jpg)](/wiki/File:Landscapes_of_the_Four_Seasons.jpg)

[Sesshū Tōyō](/wiki/Sessh%C5%AB_T%C5%8Dy%C5%8D "Sesshū Tōyō"), *Landscapes of the Four Seasons* (1486), ink and light color on paper

Ink paintings are done with a liquid that contains pigments or [dyes](/wiki/Dye "Dye") and is used to color a surface to produce an image, [text](/wiki/Writing "Writing"), or [design](/wiki/Design "Design"). Ink is used for drawing with a [pen](/wiki/Pen "Pen"), [brush](/wiki/Brush "Brush"), or [quill](/wiki/Quill "Quill"). Ink can be a complex medium, composed of [solvents](/wiki/Solvent "Solvent"), pigments, dyes, [resins](/wiki/Resin "Resin"), [lubricants](/wiki/Lubricant "Lubricant"), solubilizers, [surfactants](/wiki/Surfactant "Surfactant"), [particulate matter](/wiki/Suspended_solids "Suspended solids"), [fluorescers](/wiki/Fluorescent "Fluorescent"), and other materials. The components of inks serve many purposes; the ink's carrier, colorants, and other additives control flow and thickness of the ink and its appearance when dry.

### Enamel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Enamel")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Waddesdon_bequest_British_Museum_DSCF9814_05.JPG/250px-Waddesdon_bequest_British_Museum_DSCF9814_05.JPG)](/wiki/File:Waddesdon_bequest_British_Museum_DSCF9814_05.JPG)

[Jean de Court](/wiki/Jean_de_Court "Jean de Court") (attributed), painted [Limoges enamel](/wiki/Limoges_enamel "Limoges enamel") dish in detail (mid-16th century), [Waddesdon Bequest](/wiki/Waddesdon_Bequest "Waddesdon Bequest"), [British Museum](/wiki/British_Museum "British Museum")

[Enamels](/wiki/Vitreous_enamel "Vitreous enamel") are made by painting a substrate, typically metal, with powdered glass; minerals called color oxides provide coloration. After firing at a temperature of 750–850 degrees Celsius (1380–1560 degrees Fahrenheit), the result is a fused lamination of glass and metal. Unlike most painted techniques, the surface can be handled and wetted. Enamels have traditionally been used for decoration of precious objects,[[41]](#cite_note-42) but have also been used for other purposes. [Limoges enamel](/wiki/Limoges_enamel "Limoges enamel") was the leading centre of Renaissance enamel painting, with small religious and mythological scenes in decorated surrounds, on plaques or objects such as [salts](/wiki/Salt_cellar "Salt cellar") or caskets. In the 18th century, enamel painting enjoyed a vogue in Europe, especially as a medium for [portrait miniatures](/wiki/Portrait_miniature "Portrait miniature").[[42]](#cite_note-43) In the late 20th century, the technique of porcelain enamel on metal has been used as a durable medium for outdoor murals.[[43]](#cite_note-44)

### Tempera

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Tempera")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Sandro_Botticelli_-_La_nascita_di_Venere_-_Google_Art_Project_-_edited.jpg/250px-Sandro_Botticelli_-_La_nascita_di_Venere_-_Google_Art_Project_-_edited.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sandro_Botticelli_-_La_nascita_di_Venere_-_Google_Art_Project_-_edited.jpg)

[Sandro Botticelli](/wiki/Sandro_Botticelli "Sandro Botticelli"), *[The Birth of Venus](/wiki/The_Birth_of_Venus "The Birth of Venus")*, Tempera (1485–1486)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/The_Three_Graces%2C_by_Peter_Paul_Rubens%2C_from_Prado_in_Google_Earth.jpg/250px-The_Three_Graces%2C_by_Peter_Paul_Rubens%2C_from_Prado_in_Google_Earth.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Three_Graces,_by_Peter_Paul_Rubens,_from_Prado_in_Google_Earth.jpg)

The Three Graces (1636–1638). Museo del Prado, Madrid

[Tempera](/wiki/Tempera "Tempera"), also known as egg tempera, is a permanent, fast-drying painting medium consisting of colored pigment mixed with a water-soluble [binder](/wiki/Binder_(material) "Binder (material)") medium (usually a glutinous material such as egg yolk or some other [size](/wiki/Sizing "Sizing")). Tempera also refers to the paintings done in this medium. Tempera paintings are very long-lasting, and examples from the first centuries CE still exist. Egg tempera was a primary method of painting until after 1500 when it was superseded by the invention of [oil painting](/wiki/Oil_painting "Oil painting"). A paint commonly called tempera (though it is not) consisting of pigment and glue size is commonly used and referred to by some manufacturers in America as [poster paint](/wiki/Poster_paint "Poster paint").

### Fresco

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Fresco")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Meister_von_Mileseva_001.jpg/250px-Meister_von_Mileseva_001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Meister_von_Mileseva_001.jpg)

*[White Angel](/wiki/White_Angel "White Angel")* (fresco, c. 1235), Mileševa monastery, Serbia

[Fresco](/wiki/Fresco "Fresco") is any of several related [mural](/wiki/Mural "Mural") painting types, done on [plaster](/wiki/Plaster "Plaster") on walls or ceilings. The word fresco comes from the [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") word *affresco* [afˈfresːko], which derives from the Latin word for *fresh*. Frescoes were often made during the Renaissance and other early time periods.
*[Buon fresco](/wiki/Buon_fresco "Buon fresco")* technique consists of painting in pigment mixed with water on a thin layer of wet, fresh [lime mortar](/wiki/Mortar_(masonry)#Lime_mortar "Mortar (masonry)") or [plaster](/wiki/Plaster "Plaster"), for which the Italian word for plaster, [intonaco](/wiki/Intonaco "Intonaco"), is used. *[A secco](/wiki/A_secco "A secco")* painting, in contrast, is done on dry plaster (*secco* is "dry" in Italian). The pigments require a binding medium, such as [egg](/wiki/Egg_(food) "Egg (food)") ([tempera](/wiki/Tempera "Tempera")), glue or [oil](/wiki/Oil_painting "Oil painting") to attach the pigment to the wall.

### Oil

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Oil")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Honor%C3%A9_Daumier_008.jpg/250px-Honor%C3%A9_Daumier_008.jpg)](/wiki/File:Honor%C3%A9_Daumier_008.jpg)

[Honoré Daumier](/wiki/Honor%C3%A9_Daumier "Honoré Daumier"), *The Painter* (1808–1879), oil on panel with visible brushstrokes

[Oil painting](/wiki/Oil_painting "Oil painting") is the process of painting with [pigments](/wiki/Pigments "Pigments") that are bound with a medium of [drying oil](/wiki/Drying_oil "Drying oil"), such as [linseed oil](/wiki/Linseed_oil "Linseed oil"), [poppyseed oil](/wiki/Poppyseed_oil "Poppyseed oil") which was widely used in early modern Europe. Often the oil was boiled with a resin such as pine resin or even [frankincense](/wiki/Frankincense "Frankincense"); these were called 'varnishes' and were prized for their body and gloss. Oil paint eventually became the principal medium used for creating artworks as its advantages became widely known. The transition began with [Early Netherlandish painting](/wiki/Early_Netherlandish_painting "Early Netherlandish painting") in northern Europe, and by the height of the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") oil painting techniques had almost completely replaced [tempera](/wiki/Tempera "Tempera") paints in the majority of Europe.

### Pastel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Pastel")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Louis15-1.jpg/250px-Louis15-1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Louis15-1.jpg)

[Maurice Quentin de La Tour](/wiki/Maurice_Quentin_de_La_Tour "Maurice Quentin de La Tour"), *Portrait of Louis XV of France* (1748), pastel

[Pastel](/wiki/Pastel "Pastel") is a painting medium in the form of a stick, consisting of pure powdered pigment and a binder.[[44]](#cite_note-45) The pigments used in pastels are the same as those used to produce all colored art media, including [oil paints](/wiki/Oil_paint "Oil paint"); the binder is of a neutral hue and low [saturation](/wiki/Colorfulness#Saturation "Colorfulness"). The color effect of pastels is closer to the natural dry pigments than that of any other process.[[45]](#cite_note-46) Because the surface of a pastel painting is fragile and easily smudged, its preservation requires protective measures such as framing under glass; it may also be sprayed with a [fixative](/wiki/Fixative_(drawing) "Fixative (drawing)"). Nonetheless, when made with permanent pigments and properly cared for, a pastel painting may endure unchanged for centuries. Pastels are not susceptible, as are paintings made with a fluid medium, to the cracking and discoloration that result from changes in the color, opacity, or dimensions of the medium as it dries.

### Acrylic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Acrylic")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Jungle_Arc.jpg/250px-Jungle_Arc.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jungle_Arc.jpg)

[Ray Burggraf](/wiki/Ray_Burggraf "Ray Burggraf"), *Jungle Arc* (1998), acrylic paint on wood

[Acrylic paint](/wiki/Acrylic_paint "Acrylic paint") is fast drying paint containing pigment suspension in [acrylic](/wiki/Acrylic_resin "Acrylic resin") polymer [emulsion](/wiki/Emulsion "Emulsion"). Acrylic paints can be diluted with water but become water-resistant when dry. Depending on how much the paint is diluted (with water) or modified with acrylic gels, media, or pastes, the finished acrylic painting can resemble a [watercolor](/wiki/Watercolor_painting "Watercolor painting") or an [oil painting](/wiki/Oil_painting "Oil painting"), or have its own unique characteristics not attainable with other media. The main practical difference between most acrylics and oil paints is the inherent drying time.[[46]](#cite_note-:12-47) Oils allow for more time to blend colors and apply even glazes over under-paintings. This slow drying aspect of oil can be seen as an advantage for certain techniques but may also impede the artist's ability to work quickly. Another difference is that watercolors must be painted onto a porous surface, primarily watercolor paper. Acrylic paints can be used on many different surfaces.[[46]](#cite_note-:12-47)[[47]](#cite_note-:2-48) Both acrylic and watercolor are easy to clean up with water. Acrylic paint should be cleaned with soap and water immediately following use. Watercolor paint can be cleaned with just water.[[48]](#cite_note-49)[[49]](#cite_note-Appellof92-50)[[50]](#cite_note-51)

Between 1946 and 1949, [Leonard Bocour](/wiki/Leonard_Bocour "Leonard Bocour") and [Sam Golden](/wiki/Sam_Golden "Sam Golden") invented a solution acrylic paint under the brand [Magna paint](/wiki/Magna_paint "Magna paint"). These were [mineral spirit](/wiki/Mineral_spirits "Mineral spirits")-based paints. Water-based acrylic paints were subsequently sold as [latex](/wiki/Latex "Latex") house paints.[[51]](#cite_note-52) In 1963, George Rowney (part of [Daler-Rowney](/wiki/Daler-Rowney "Daler-Rowney") since 1983) was the first manufacturer to introduce artists' acrylic paints in Europe, under the brand name "Cryla".[[52]](#cite_note-Art_Materials-53) Acrylics are the most common paints used in [grattage](/wiki/Grattage "Grattage"), a surrealist technique that began to be used with the advent of this type of paint. Acrylics are used for this purpose because they easily scrape or peel from a surface.[[53]](#cite_note-54)

### Spray paint

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Spray paint")]

[Aerosol paint](/wiki/Aerosol_paint "Aerosol paint") (also called spray paint)[[54]](#cite_note-55) is a type of paint that comes in a sealed pressurized container and is released in a fine spray mist when depressing a [valve](/wiki/Valve "Valve") button. A form of [spray painting](/wiki/Spray_painting "Spray painting"), [aerosol](/wiki/Aerosol "Aerosol") paint leaves a smooth, evenly coated surface. Standard sized cans are portable, inexpensive and easy to store. Aerosol [primer](/wiki/Primer_(paint) "Primer (paint)") can be applied directly to bare metal and many plastics.

Speed, portability and permanence also make aerosol paint a common [graffiti](/wiki/Graffiti "Graffiti") medium. In the late 1970s, street graffiti writers' signatures and murals became more elaborate, and a unique style developed as a factor of the aerosol medium and the speed required for illicit work. Many now recognize graffiti and street art as a unique art form and specifically manufactured aerosol paints are made for the graffiti artist. A [stencil](/wiki/Stencil "Stencil") protects a surface, except the specific shape to be painted. Stencils can be purchased as movable letters, ordered as professionally cut [logos](/wiki/Logo "Logo") or hand-cut by artists.

### Water miscible oil paint

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Water miscible oil paint")]

[Water miscible oil paints](/wiki/Water_miscible_oil_paint "Water miscible oil paint") (also called "water soluble" or "water-mixable") is a modern variety of [oil paint](/wiki/Oil_paint "Oil paint") engineered to be thinned and cleaned up with water,[[55]](#cite_note-56)[[56]](#cite_note-Dye2001-57) rather than having to use chemicals such as [turpentine](/wiki/Turpentine "Turpentine"). It can be mixed and applied using the same techniques as traditional oil-based paint, but while still wet it can be effectively removed from brushes, palettes, and rags with ordinary soap and water. Its water solubility comes from the use of an [oil](/wiki/Oil "Oil") medium in which one end of the [molecule](/wiki/Molecule "Molecule") has been altered to bind loosely to water molecules, as in a [solution](/wiki/Solution_(chemistry) "Solution (chemistry)").[[57]](#cite_note-cold_oil-58)

### Sand

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Sand")]

Main article: [Sandpainting](/wiki/Sandpainting "Sandpainting")

Sandpainting is the art of pouring coloured sands, and powdered pigments from minerals or crystals, or pigments from other natural or synthetic sources onto a surface to make a fixed or unfixed sand painting.

### Digital painting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Digital painting")]

Main article: [Digital painting](/wiki/Digital_painting "Digital painting")

Digital painting is a method of creating an art object (painting) digitally or a technique for making digital art on the computer. As a method of creating an art object, it adapts traditional painting medium such as [acrylic paint](/wiki/Acrylic_paint "Acrylic paint"), [oils](/wiki/Oil_paint "Oil paint"), [ink](/wiki/Ink "Ink"), [watercolor](/wiki/Watercolor "Watercolor"), etc. and applies the pigment to traditional carriers, such as woven canvas cloth, paper, polyester, etc. by means of [software](/wiki/Software "Software") driving [industrial robotic](/wiki/Industrial_robot "Industrial robot") or office machinery (printers). As a technique, it refers to a [computer graphics](/wiki/Computer_graphics "Computer graphics") software program that uses a [virtual](/wiki/Virtuality "Virtuality") canvas and virtual painting box of brushes, colors, and other supplies. The virtual box contains many instruments that do not exist outside the computer, and which give a [digital artwork](/wiki/Digital_art "Digital art") a different look and feel from an artwork that is made the traditional way. Furthermore, digital painting is not 'computer-generated' art as the computer does not automatically create images on the screen using some mathematical calculations. On the other hand, the artist uses his own painting technique to create a particular piece of work on the computer.[[58]](#cite_note-59)

### Other media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Other media")]

Bodily fluids have been used as painting media. [Andy Warhol](/wiki/Andy_Warhol "Andy Warhol") produced his *Oxidization* series by covering canvases with metallic paint and having his assistants and friends urinate on the still-wet paint.[[59]](#cite_note-60) Blood from menstrual periods has been used to paint images.[[60]](#cite_note-61) Sarah Maple, a contemporary artist, has used her menstrual blood to create portraits to help erase the taboo covering the topic of periods.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Painting styles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Painting styles")]

Main article: [Style (visual arts)](/wiki/Style_(visual_arts) "Style (visual arts)")

*Style* is used in two senses: It can refer to the distinctive visual elements, techniques, and methods that typify an *individual* artist's work. It can also refer to the [movement](/wiki/Art_movement "Art movement") or school that an artist is associated with. This can stem from an actual group that the artist was consciously involved with or it can be a category in which art historians have placed the painter. The word 'style' in the latter sense has fallen out of favor in academic discussions about contemporary painting, though it continues to be used in popular contexts. Such movements or classifications include the following:

### Western

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Western")]

#### Modernism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Modernism")]

[Modernism](/wiki/Modernism "Modernism") describes both a set of cultural tendencies and an array of associated [cultural movements](/wiki/Cultural_movement "Cultural movement"), originally arising from wide-scale and far-reaching changes to [Western society](/wiki/Western_culture "Western culture") in the late 19th century and early 20th century. Modernism was a revolt against the conservative values of [realism](/wiki/Realism_(arts) "Realism (arts)").[[61]](#cite_note-Barth79Replenishment-62)[[62]](#cite_note-Graff75-63) The term encompasses the activities and output of those who felt the "traditional" forms of art, architecture, literature, religious faith, social organization, and daily life were becoming outdated in the new economic, social, and political conditions of an emerging fully industrialized world. A salient characteristic of modernism is self-consciousness. This often led to experiments with form, and work that draws attention to the processes and materials used (and to the further tendency of abstraction).[[63]](#cite_note-64)

#### Impressionism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Impressionism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Monet_-_Impression%2C_Sunrise.jpg/250px-Monet_-_Impression%2C_Sunrise.jpg)](/wiki/File:Monet_-_Impression,_Sunrise.jpg)

[Claude Monet](/wiki/Claude_Monet "Claude Monet")'s 1872 *[Impression, Sunrise](/wiki/Impression,_Sunrise "Impression, Sunrise")* inspired the name of [the movement](/wiki/Impressionism "Impressionism")

The first example of modernism in painting was [impressionism](/wiki/Impressionism "Impressionism"), a school of painting that initially focused on work done, not in studios, but outdoors (*[en plein air](/wiki/En_plein_air "En plein air")*). Impressionist paintings demonstrated that human beings do not see objects, but instead see light itself. The school gathered adherents despite internal divisions among its leading practitioners and became increasingly influential. Initially rejected from the most important commercial show of the time, the government-sponsored [Paris Salon](/wiki/Paris_Salon "Paris Salon"), the [Impressionists](/wiki/Impressionists "Impressionists") organized yearly group exhibitions in commercial venues during the 1870s and 1880s, timing them to coincide with the official Salon. A significant event of 1863 was the [Salon des Refusés](/wiki/Salon_des_Refus%C3%A9s "Salon des Refusés"), created by [Emperor Napoleon III](/wiki/Napoleon_III_of_France "Napoleon III of France") to display all of the paintings rejected by the Paris Salon.

#### Abstract styles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Abstract styles")]

[Abstract painting](/wiki/Abstract_art "Abstract art") uses a [visual language](/wiki/Visual_language "Visual language") of form, colour and line to create a composition that may exist with a degree of independence from visual references in the world.[[64]](#cite_note-65)[[65]](#cite_note-66) [Abstract expressionism](/wiki/Abstract_expressionism "Abstract expressionism") was an American post-[World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") [art movement](/wiki/Art_movement "Art movement") that combined the emotional intensity and self-denial of the German [Expressionists](/wiki/Expressionism "Expressionism") with the anti-figurative aesthetic of the European abstract schools—such as [Futurism](/wiki/Futurism_(art) "Futurism (art)"), [Bauhaus](/wiki/Bauhaus "Bauhaus") and [Cubism](/wiki/Cubism "Cubism"), and the image of being rebellious, anarchic, highly idiosyncratic and, some feel, nihilistic.[[66]](#cite_note-67)

[Action painting](/wiki/Action_painting "Action painting"), sometimes called *gestural abstraction*, is a style of painting in which paint is spontaneously dribbled, splashed or smeared onto the canvas, rather than being carefully applied.[[67]](#cite_note-68) The resulting work often emphasizes the physical act of painting itself as an essential aspect of the finished work or concern of its artist. The style was widespread from the 1940s until the early 1960s and is closely associated with [abstract expressionism](/wiki/Abstract_expressionism "Abstract expressionism") (some critics have used the terms "action painting" and "abstract expressionism" interchangeably).

Other modernist styles include:

* [Color Field](/wiki/Color_Field "Color Field")
* [Lyrical Abstraction](/wiki/Lyrical_Abstraction "Lyrical Abstraction")
* [Hard-edge painting](/wiki/Hard-edge_painting "Hard-edge painting")
* [Pop art](/wiki/Pop_art "Pop art")

#### Outsider art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Outsider art")]

The term [outsider art](/wiki/Outsider_art "Outsider art") was coined by [art critic](/wiki/Art_critic "Art critic") Roger Cardinal in 1972 as an English synonym for art brut (French: [[aʁ bʁyt]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French"), "raw art" or "rough art"), a label created by [French](/wiki/France "France") [artist](/wiki/Artist "Artist") [Jean Dubuffet](/wiki/Jean_Dubuffet "Jean Dubuffet") to describe [art](/wiki/Art "Art") created outside the boundaries of official culture; Dubuffet focused particularly on art by [insane-asylum](/wiki/Psychiatric_hospital "Psychiatric hospital") inmates.[[68]](#cite_note-69) Outsider art has emerged as a successful art marketing category (an annual Outsider Art Fair has taken place in New York since 1992). The term is sometimes misapplied as a catch-all marketing label for art created by people outside the mainstream "art world", regardless of their circumstances or the content of their work.

#### Photorealism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Photorealism")]

[Photorealism](/wiki/Photorealism "Photorealism") is the genre of painting based on using the camera and photographs to gather information and then from this information, creating a painting that appears to be very realistic like a [photograph](/wiki/Photograph "Photograph"). The term is primarily applied to paintings from the United States [art movement](/wiki/Art_movement "Art movement") that began in the late 1960s and early 1970s. As a full-fledged art movement, Photorealism evolved from [Pop Art](/wiki/Pop_Art "Pop Art")[[69]](#cite_note-Supperrealist_Pop_Art-70)[[70]](#cite_note-Chase,_Linda_2002._pp_14-15-71)[[71]](#cite_note-72) and as a counter to [Abstract Expressionism](/wiki/Abstract_Expressionism "Abstract Expressionism").

[Hyperrealism](/wiki/Hyperrealism_(painting) "Hyperrealism (painting)") is a genre of painting and sculpture resembling a high-resolution [photograph](/wiki/Photograph "Photograph"). [Hyperrealism](/wiki/Hyperrealism_(painting) "Hyperrealism (painting)") is a fully-fledged school of [art](/wiki/Art "Art") and can be considered an advancement of Photorealism by the methods used to create the resulting paintings or sculptures. The term is primarily applied to an independent art movement and art style in the United States and Europe that has developed since the early 2000s.[[72]](#cite_note-73)

#### Surrealism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Surrealism")]

[Surrealism](/wiki/Surrealism "Surrealism") is a cultural movement that began in the early 1920s and is best known for the artistic and literary production of those affiliated with the [Surrealist Movement](/wiki/Surrealist_Movement "Surrealist Movement"). Surrealist artworks feature the element of surprise, the uncanny, the unconscious, unexpected juxtapositions and [non-sequitur](/wiki/Non_sequitur_(literary_device) "Non sequitur (literary device)"); however, many Surrealist artists and writers regard their work as an expression of the philosophical movement first and foremost, with the works being an artifact. Leader [André Breton](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Breton "André Breton") was explicit in his assertion that Surrealism was above all a revolutionary movement.

Surrealism developed out of the [Dada](/wiki/Dada "Dada") activities of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") and the most important center of the movement was [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"). From the 1920s onward, the movement spread around the globe, eventually affecting the [visual arts](/wiki/Visual_art "Visual art"), [literature](/wiki/Literature "Literature"), [film](/wiki/Film "Film") and [music](/wiki/Music "Music") of many countries, as well as [political](/wiki/Politics "Politics") thought and practice, [philosophy](/wiki/Philosophy "Philosophy") and [social theory](/wiki/Social_theory "Social theory").

See also: [Outline of painting § Styles of painting](/wiki/Outline_of_painting#Styles_of_painting "Outline of painting")

### Indian

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Indian")]

#### Miniature painting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Miniature painting")]

Miniature paintings were the primary form of painting in pre-colonial India. These were done on a special paper (known as wasli) using mineral and natural colours. Miniature painting is not one style but a group of several styles of schools of painting such as Mughal, Pahari, Rajasthani, Company style etc.

**[Mughal miniature painting](/wiki/Mughal_painting "Mughal painting")** is a particular style of [South Asian](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia"), particularly North Indian (more specifically, modern day India and Pakistan), painting confined to [miniatures](/wiki/Miniature_(illuminated_manuscript) "Miniature (illuminated manuscript)") either as book illustrations or as single works to be kept in albums ([muraqqa](/wiki/Muraqqa "Muraqqa")). It emerged[[73]](#cite_note-74) from [Persian miniature](/wiki/Persian_miniature "Persian miniature") painting (itself partly of [Chinese origin](/wiki/Chinese_painting "Chinese painting")) and developed in the court of the [Mughal Empire](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire") of the 16th to 18th centuries. Mughal painting immediately took a much greater interest in realistic portraiture than was typical of Persian miniatures. Animals and plants were the main subject of many miniatures for albums, and were more realistically depicted.[[74]](#cite_note-75)[[75]](#cite_note-76)[[76]](#cite_note-77)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Maker_unknown%2C_India_-_Krishna_and_Radha_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-Maker_unknown%2C_India_-_Krishna_and_Radha_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maker_unknown,_India_-_Krishna_and_Radha_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

*Krishna and Radha*, might be the work of [Nihâl Chand](/wiki/Nih%C3%A2l_Chand "Nihâl Chand"), master of Kishangarh school of Rajput Painting

**[Rajasthani painting](/wiki/Rajasthani_painting "Rajasthani painting")** evolved and flourished in the royal courts of [Rajputana](/wiki/Rajputana "Rajputana")[[77]](#cite_note-78) in northern India, mainly during the 17th century. Artists trained in the tradition of the [Mughal miniature](/wiki/Mughal_miniature "Mughal miniature") were dispersed from the imperial Mughal court, and developed styles also drawing from local traditions of painting, especially those illustrating the Sanskrit Epics, the *[Mahabharata](/wiki/Mahabharata "Mahabharata")* and *[Ramayana](/wiki/Ramayana "Ramayana")*. Subjects varied, but portraits of the ruling family, often engaged in hunting or their daily activities, were generally popular, as were narrative scenes from the epics or [Hindu mythology](/wiki/Hindu_mythology "Hindu mythology"), as well as some [genre scenes](/wiki/Genre_painting "Genre painting") of landscapes, and humans.[[78]](#cite_note-79)[[79]](#cite_note-80)

**Punjab Hills or [Pahari painting](/wiki/Pahari_painting "Pahari painting")** of which Kangra, Guller, Basholi were major sub-styles. Kangra painting is the pictorial art of [Kangra](/wiki/Kangra,_Himachal_Pradesh "Kangra, Himachal Pradesh"), named after [Kangra](/wiki/Kangra_State "Kangra State"), [Himachal Pradesh](/wiki/Himachal_Pradesh "Himachal Pradesh"), a former [princely state](/wiki/Princely_state "Princely state"), which patronized the art. It became prevalent with the fading of [Basohli school of painting](/wiki/Basohli_Painting "Basohli Painting") in mid-18th century.[[80]](#cite_note-81)[[81]](#cite_note-82) The focal theme of Kangra painting is Shringar (the erotic sentiment). The subjects are seen in Kangra painting exhibit the taste and the traits of the lifestyle of the society of that period.[[82]](#cite_note-83) The artists adopted themes from the love poetry of [Jayadeva](/wiki/Jayadeva "Jayadeva") and [Keshav Das](/wiki/Keshavdas "Keshavdas") who wrote ecstatically of the love of [Radha](/wiki/Radha "Radha") and [Krishna](/wiki/Krishna "Krishna") with [Bhakti](/wiki/Bhakti "Bhakti") being the driving force.[[83]](#cite_note-84)[[84]](#cite_note-85)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Khan_Bahadur_Khan_with_men_of_his_clan_%286125079998%29_cropped.jpg/250px-Khan_Bahadur_Khan_with_men_of_his_clan_%286125079998%29_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Khan_Bahadur_Khan_with_men_of_his_clan_(6125079998)_cropped.jpg)

Khan Bahadur Khan with Men of his Clan, c. 1815, from the Fraser Album, Company Style

**Company style** is a term for a hybrid Indo-European style of paintings made in India by Indian artists, many of whom worked for European patrons in the [British East India Company](/wiki/British_East_India_Company "British East India Company") or other foreign Companies in the 18th and 19th centuries.[[85]](#cite_note-86) Three distinct styles of Company Painting emerged in three British Power Centres – [Delhi](/wiki/Delhi "Delhi"), [Calcutta](/wiki/Kolkata "Kolkata") and [Madras](/wiki/Madras "Madras"). The subject matter of company paintings made for western patrons was often documentary rather than imaginative, and as a consequence, the Indian artists were required to adopt a more naturalistic approach to painting than had traditionally been usual.[[86]](#cite_note-87)[[87]](#cite_note-88)

The **Sikh style** and [**Deccan style**](/wiki/Deccan_painting "Deccan painting") are other prominent Miniature painting styles of India.

#### Pichwai painting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Pichwai painting")]

[Pichwai paintings](/wiki/Pichhwai "Pichhwai") are paintings on textile and usually depicting stories from the life of Lord Krishna.[[88]](#cite_note-89) These were made in large format and often used as a backdrop to the main idol in temples or homes. Pichwai paintings were made and are still made mainly in Rajasthan, India. However very few were made in the Deccan region, but these are extremely rare. The purpose of pichhwais, other than artistic appeal, is to narrate tales of Krishna to the illiterate. Temples have sets with different images, which are changed according to the [calendar of festivals celebrating the deity](/wiki/Shrinathji#Festivals_and_rituals_at_the_temple "Shrinathji").[[89]](#cite_note-90)

#### Folk and tribal art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Folk and tribal art")]

Pattachitra is a general term for traditional, cloth-based [scroll](/wiki/Scroll "Scroll") painting, based in the eastern Indian [states](/wiki/States_and_territories_of_India "States and territories of India") of [Odisha](/wiki/Odisha "Odisha") and [West Bengal](/wiki/West_Bengal "West Bengal").[[90]](#cite_note-91) The Pattachitra painting tradition is closely linked with the worship of Lord [Jagannath](/wiki/Jagannath "Jagannath") in Odisha.[[91]](#cite_note-92) The subject matter of Pattachitra is limited to religious themes. Patachitra artform is known for its intricate details as well as mythological narratives and folktales inscribed in it. All colours used in the Paintings are natural and paintings are made fully old traditional way by Chitrakaras that is Odiya Painter. Pattachitra style of painting is one of the oldest and most popular art forms of [Odisha](/wiki/Odisha "Odisha"). Patachitras are a component of an ancient [Bengali](/wiki/Bengalis "Bengalis") narrative art, originally serving as a visual device during the performance of a song.[[92]](#cite_note-93)[[93]](#cite_note-94)[[94]](#cite_note-95)

Madhubani Art is a style of [Indian painting](/wiki/Indian_painting "Indian painting"), practiced in the [Mithila region](/wiki/Mithila_(region) "Mithila (region)") of India and Nepal. The style is characterized by complex geometrical patterns, these paintings are famous for representing ritual content used for particular occasions like festivals, religious rituals etc.[[95]](#cite_note-96)

[Warli](/wiki/Warli_painting "Warli painting") is another folk tribal art form from India.

#### Bengal School

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Bengal School")]

The Bengal School[[96]](#cite_note-97) was an [art movement](/wiki/Art_movement "Art movement") and a style of [Indian painting](/wiki/Indian_painting "Indian painting") that originated in [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal "Bengal"), primarily [Kolkata](/wiki/Kolkata "Kolkata") and [Shantiniketan](/wiki/Shantiniketan "Shantiniketan"), and flourished throughout the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent"), during the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") in the early 20th century.[[97]](#cite_note-98) The Bengal school arose as an [avant garde](/wiki/Avant_garde "Avant garde") and nationalist movement reacting against the [academic art](/wiki/Academic_art "Academic art") styles previously promoted in India, both by Indian artists such as [Raja Ravi Varma](/wiki/Raja_Ravi_Varma "Raja Ravi Varma") and in British art schools. The school wanted to establish a distinct Indian style which celebrated the indigenous cultural heritage. In an attempt to reject colonial aesthetics, [Abanindranath Tagore](/wiki/Abanindranath_Tagore "Abanindranath Tagore") also turned to China and Japan with the intent of promoting a pan-Asian aesthetic and incorporated elements from Far Eastern art, such as the [Japanese wash technique](/wiki/Ink_wash_painting "Ink wash painting").[[98]](#cite_note-99)[[99]](#cite_note-100)[[100]](#cite_note-101)
