## Types of painting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Types of painting")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Bodeg%C3%B3n_de_recipientes_%28Zurbar%C3%A1n%29.jpg/250px-Bodeg%C3%B3n_de_recipientes_%28Zurbar%C3%A1n%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bodeg%C3%B3n_de_recipientes_(Zurbar%C3%A1n).jpg)

[Francisco de Zurbarán](/wiki/Francisco_de_Zurbar%C3%A1n "Francisco de Zurbarán"), *Still Life with Pottery Jars* ([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Bodegón de recipientes*) (1636), oil on canvas, 46 x 84 cm, [Museo del Prado](/wiki/Museo_del_Prado "Museo del Prado"), [Madrid](/wiki/Madrid "Madrid")

### Allegory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Allegory")]

[Allegory](/wiki/Allegory "Allegory") is a [figurative](/wiki/Figurative_art "Figurative art") [mode](/wiki/Mode_(literature) "Mode (literature)") of representation conveying meaning other than the literal. Allegory communicates its message by means of [symbolic](/wiki/Symbol "Symbol") figures, actions, or symbolic representation. Allegory is generally treated as a figure of [rhetoric](/wiki/Rhetoric "Rhetoric"), but an allegory does not have to be expressed in [language](/wiki/Language "Language"): it may be addressed to the eye and is often found in realistic painting. An example of a simple visual allegory is the image of the [grim reaper](/wiki/Personifications_of_death "Personifications of death"). Viewers understand that the image of the grim reaper is a symbolic representation of death.

### Bodegón

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Bodegón")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Reza_Abbasi_-_Two_Lovers_%281630%29.jpg/250px-Reza_Abbasi_-_Two_Lovers_%281630%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Reza_Abbasi_-_Two_Lovers_(1630).jpg)

[Reza Abbasi](/wiki/Reza_Abbasi "Reza Abbasi"), *Two Lovers* (1630)

In [Spanish art](/wiki/Spanish_art "Spanish art"), a [bodegón](/wiki/Bodeg%C3%B3n "Bodegón") is a [still life](/wiki/Still_life "Still life") painting depicting pantry items, such as victuals, game, and drink, often arranged on a simple stone slab, and also a painting with one or more figures, but significant still life elements, typically set in a kitchen or tavern. Starting in the [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque "Baroque") period, such paintings became popular in Spain in the second quarter of the 17th century. The tradition of still life painting appears to have started and was far more popular in the contemporary [Low Countries](/wiki/Low_Countries "Low Countries"), today Belgium and [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") (then Flemish and Dutch artists), than it ever was in [southern Europe](/wiki/Southern_Europe "Southern Europe"). [Northern still lifes](/wiki/Dutch_Golden_Age_painting#Still_lifes "Dutch Golden Age painting") had many subgenres: the *breakfast piece* was augmented by the *[trompe-l'œil](/wiki/Trompe-l%27%C5%93il "Trompe-l'œil")*, the *flower bouquet*, and the *[vanitas](/wiki/Vanitas "Vanitas")*. In Spain, there were much fewer patrons for this sort of thing, but a type of *breakfast piece* did become popular, featuring a few objects of food and tableware laid on a table.

### Figure painting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Figure painting")]

A [figure painting](/wiki/Figure_painting "Figure painting") is a [work of art](/wiki/Work_of_art "Work of art") in any of the painting media with the primary subject being the human figure, whether clothed or [nude](/wiki/Nude_(art) "Nude (art)").
Figure painting may also refer to the activity of creating such a work. The human figure has been one of the contrast subjects of art since the first Stone Age cave paintings and has been reinterpreted in various styles throughout history.[[102]](#cite_note-103) Some artists well known for figure painting are [Peter Paul Rubens](/wiki/Peter_Paul_Rubens "Peter Paul Rubens"), [Edgar Degas](/wiki/Edgar_Degas "Edgar Degas"), and [Édouard Manet](/wiki/%C3%89douard_Manet "Édouard Manet").

### Illustration painting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Illustration painting")]

[Illustration](/wiki/Illustration "Illustration") paintings are those used as illustrations in books, magazines, and theater or movie [posters](/wiki/Posters "Posters") and comic books. Today, there is a growing interest in collecting and admiring the original artwork. Various museum exhibitions, magazines, and art galleries have devoted space to the illustrators of the past. In the visual art world, illustrators have sometimes been considered less important in comparison with fine artists and [graphic designers](/wiki/Graphic_designer "Graphic designer"). But as the result of [computer game](/wiki/Computer_game "Computer game") and comic industry growth, illustrations are becoming valued as popular and profitable artworks that can acquire a wider market than the other two, especially in [Korea](/wiki/Korea "Korea"), Japan, [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") and the United States.

The illustrations of medieval [codices](/wiki/Codices "Codices") were known as [illuminations](/wiki/Illuminated_manuscript "Illuminated manuscript"), and were individually hand-drawn and painted. With the invention of the [printing press](/wiki/Printing_press "Printing press") during the 15th century, [books](/wiki/Books "Books") became more widely distributed, and often illustrated with [woodcuts](/wiki/Woodcuts "Woodcuts").[[103]](#cite_note-104)[[104]](#cite_note-105) In [America](/wiki/America "America"), this led to a "golden age of illustration" from before the 1880s until the early 20th century. A small group of illustrators became highly successful, with the imagery they created considered a portrait of American aspirations of the time.[[105]](#cite_note-106) Among the best-known illustrators of that period were [N.C. Wyeth](/wiki/N._C._Wyeth "N. C. Wyeth") and [Howard Pyle](/wiki/Howard_Pyle "Howard Pyle") of the [Brandywine School](/wiki/Brandywine_School "Brandywine School"), [James Montgomery Flagg](/wiki/James_Montgomery_Flagg "James Montgomery Flagg"), [Elizabeth Shippen Green](/wiki/Elizabeth_Shippen_Green "Elizabeth Shippen Green"), [J. C. Leyendecker](/wiki/J._C._Leyendecker "J. C. Leyendecker"), [Violet Oakley](/wiki/Violet_Oakley "Violet Oakley"), [Maxfield Parrish](/wiki/Maxfield_Parrish "Maxfield Parrish"), [Jessie Willcox Smith](/wiki/Jessie_Willcox_Smith "Jessie Willcox Smith"), and [John Rea Neill](/wiki/John_Rea_Neill "John Rea Neill"). In [France](/wiki/France "France"), on 1905, the Contemporary Book Society commissioned [Paul Jouve](/wiki/Paul_Jouve "Paul Jouve") to illustrate [Rudyard Kipling's Jungle Book](/wiki/Rudyard_Kipling%27s_The_Jungle_Book "Rudyard Kipling's The Jungle Book"). Paul Jouve will devote ten years to the 130 illustrations of this book which will remain as one of the masterpieces of bibliophilia.[[106]](#cite_note-107)

### Landscape painting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: Landscape painting")]

Main article: [Landscape art](/wiki/Landscape_art "Landscape art")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Andreas_Achenbach_-_Clearing_Up%E2%80%94Coast_of_Sicily_-_Walters_37116.jpg/250px-Andreas_Achenbach_-_Clearing_Up%E2%80%94Coast_of_Sicily_-_Walters_37116.jpg)](/wiki/File:Andreas_Achenbach_-_Clearing_Up%E2%80%94Coast_of_Sicily_-_Walters_37116.jpg)

[Andreas Achenbach](/wiki/Andreas_Achenbach "Andreas Achenbach"), *Clearing Up, Coast of Sicily* (1847), [The Walters Art Museum](/wiki/The_Walters_Art_Museum "The Walters Art Museum")[[107]](#cite_note-108)[[108]](#cite_note-109)

[Landscape painting](/wiki/Landscape_art "Landscape art") is a term that covers the depiction of natural scenery such as mountains, valleys, trees, rivers, lakes, and forests, and especially art where the main subject is a wide view, with its elements arranged into a coherent composition. In other works, landscape backgrounds for figures can still form an important part of the work. The sky is almost always included in the view, and [weather](/wiki/Weather "Weather") is often an element of the composition. Detailed landscapes as a distinct subject are not found in all artistic traditions and develop when there is already a sophisticated tradition of representing other subjects. The two main traditions spring from [Western painting](/wiki/Western_painting "Western painting") and [Chinese art](/wiki/Chinese_art "Chinese art"), going back well over a thousand years in both cases.

### Portrait painting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=55 "Edit section: Portrait painting")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Abraham_Lincoln_in_the_United_States_Congress_by.jpg/250px-Abraham_Lincoln_in_the_United_States_Congress_by.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abraham_Lincoln_in_the_United_States_Congress_by.jpg)

[Ned Bittinger](/wiki/Ned_Bittinger "Ned Bittinger"), *[Portrait of Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln_(Bittinger) "Abraham Lincoln (Bittinger)") in Congress* (2004), [US Capitol](/wiki/United_States_Capitol "United States Capitol")

[Portrait paintings](/wiki/Portrait_painting "Portrait painting") are representations of a person, in which the face and its expression is predominant. The intent is to display the likeness, [personality](/wiki/Personality_type "Personality type"), and even the mood of the person. The art of the portrait flourished in Ancient Greek and especially [Roman sculpture](/wiki/Roman_sculpture "Roman sculpture"), where sitters demanded individualized and realistic portraits, even unflattering ones. One of the best-known portraits in the Western world is [Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci")'s painting titled *[Mona Lisa](/wiki/Mona_Lisa "Mona Lisa")*, which is thought to be a portrait of [Lisa Gherardini](/wiki/Lisa_del_Giocondo "Lisa del Giocondo"), the wife of Francesco del Giocondo.[[109]](#cite_note-110)

Warhol was one of the most prolific portrait painters of the 20th century. Warhol's painting *[Orange Shot Marilyn](/wiki/Shot_Marilyns "Shot Marilyns")* of [Marilyn Monroe](/wiki/Marilyn_Monroe "Marilyn Monroe") is an iconic early example of his work from the 1960s, and [Orange Prince (1984)](/wiki/Prince_(painting) "Prince (painting)") of the pop singer [Prince](/wiki/Prince_(musician) "Prince (musician)") is later example, both exhibiting Warhol's unique graphic style of portraiture.[[110]](#cite_note-111)[[111]](#cite_note-112)[[112]](#cite_note-113)

### Still life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=56 "Edit section: Still life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Otto_Marseus_van_Schrieck_-_A_Forest_Floor_Still-Life_-_WGA21061.jpg/250px-Otto_Marseus_van_Schrieck_-_A_Forest_Floor_Still-Life_-_WGA21061.jpg)](/wiki/File:Otto_Marseus_van_Schrieck_-_A_Forest_Floor_Still-Life_-_WGA21061.jpg)

[Otto Marseus van Schrieck](/wiki/Otto_Marseus_van_Schrieck "Otto Marseus van Schrieck"), *A Forest Floor Still-Life* (1666)

A [still life](/wiki/Still_life "Still life") is a work of [art](/wiki/Art "Art") depicting mostly [inanimate](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/inanimate "wikt:inanimate") subject matter, typically commonplace objects—which may be either natural (food, flowers, plants, rocks, or shells) or human-made (drinking glasses, books, vases, jewelry, coins, pipes, and so on). With origins in the Middle Ages and Ancient Greek/Roman art, still life paintings give the artist more leeway in the arrangement of design elements within a composition than do paintings of other types of subjects such as [landscape](/wiki/Landscape_art "Landscape art") or [portraiture](/wiki/Portrait "Portrait"). Still life paintings, particularly before 1700, often contained religious and allegorical symbolism relating to the objects depicted. Some modern still life breaks the two-dimensional barrier and employs three-dimensional mixed media, and uses found objects, photography, computer graphics, as well as video and sound.

### Veduta

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Painting&action=edit&section=57 "Edit section: Veduta")]

A [veduta](/wiki/Veduta "Veduta") is a highly detailed, usually large-scale painting of a [cityscape](/wiki/Cityscape "Cityscape") or some other vista. This [genre](/wiki/Genre "Genre") of [landscape](/wiki/Landscape_art "Landscape art") originated in [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders"), where artists such as [Paul Bril](/wiki/Paul_Bril "Paul Bril") painted *vedute* as early as the 16th century. As the itinerary of the [Grand Tour](/wiki/Grand_Tour "Grand Tour") became somewhat standardized, *vedute* of familiar scenes like the Roman Forum or the Grand Canal recalled early ventures to the Continent for aristocratic Englishmen. In the later 19th century, more personal impressions of cityscapes replaced the desire for topographical accuracy, which was satisfied instead by painted [panoramas](/wiki/Panorama "Panorama").

   "using uranium-series dating of coralloid speleothems directly associated with 12 human hand stencils and two figurative animal depictions from seven cave sites in the Maros karsts of Sulawesi, we show that rock art traditions on this Indonesian island are at least compatible in age with the oldest European art. The earliest dated image from Maros, with a minimum age of 39.9 kyr, is now the oldest known hand stencil in the world. In addition, a painting of a [babirusa ('pig-deer')](/wiki/Babirusa "Babirusa") made at least 35.4 kyr ago is among the earliest dated figurative depictions worldwide, if not the earliest one. Among the implications, it can now be demonstrated that humans were producing rock art by ~40 kyr ago at opposite ends of the Pleistocene Eurasian world."
